title: Roof space ventilation and insulation: getting the balance right
subtitle: Why insulating at ceiling level still needs eaves ventilation
excerpt: Insulating a cold loft at floor level requires ventilation in the roof space above the insulation to prevent condensation on the roof deck. Block the eaves accidentally and you will have damp timbers within two heating seasons.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Part F (2022) for ventilation requirements in roof spaces. BS 5250 Code of Practice for Control of Condensation in Buildings. NHBC Standards Chapter 7.2 on roof ventilation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A membrane that slows the movement of water vapour through a building element. Required on the warm side of insulation in many assemblies to prevent interstitial condensation.
Glass or rock fibre insulation supplied as batts, rolls, or loose fill. The standard loft-insulation material in the UK, with a lambda value of around 0.040 W/mK.

content.text: A cold roof design insulates at ceiling level and leaves the roof space above the insulation ventilated with outside air. The ventilation serves one purpose: to remove water vapour that rises from the habitable rooms below through the ceiling and through the
vapour control layer

. If ventilation is blocked, vapour builds up in the roof space and condenses on the cold roof deck. This causes timber decay, mould, and structural damage. It is the most common reason loft timbers fail after insulation work.
How ventilation works in a cold roof

In a standard pitched roof, eaves vents at the bottom of the slope allow outside air to enter. The air rises up the slope under the tiles and exits at the ridge or through ridge vents. This cross-ventilation keeps the roof space at near-outdoor temperature and humidity, preventing condensation on the roof deck.

Part F of the Building Regulations requires a gap of at least 25 mm between the insulation surface and the roof deck at the eaves. For 250 mm of insulation in a 100 mm joist, the insulation sits 150 mm above the joist top and the 25 mm gap must be kept to the rafter soffit. Eaves baffles hold the insulation back from the eaves and stop the rolls slumping into the gap over time.
The blocked-eaves problem
When a
mineral wool

roll is pushed right to the eaves without a baffle, it fills the ventilation gap and blocks the airflow path. Within two or three heating seasons, condensation typically appears on the rafters nearest the eaves. This is often the first visible sign that the ventilation has been lost. By this point, the timber may already have been wet for long enough to support mould growth.
Fitting eaves baffles
Access the eaves from inside the loft. You will need a board or crawling plank over the joists: do not put your weight on the ceiling between the joists.
Check whether any baffles are already in place. Polystyrene or cardboard baffles from a previous insulation installation may be present but collapsed or missing. Replace damaged baffles.

Cut plastic or polystyrene baffles to fit between each pair of rafters. The baffle should run from the eaves upward at least 300 mm. Slide the baffle up between the rafters with the shaped edge pointing inward and the straight edge toward the outside. The baffle holds a 50 mm ventilation channel between itself and the underside of the sarking felt.
Roll the insulation into the bay up to the baffle. Do not push it past the baffle into the eaves zone. The insulation stops where the baffle starts.

Check the eaves ventilation path from below. You should be able to see daylight through the eaves vent along the baffle channel. If the fascia board blocks the vent slot, fit proprietary eaves ventilation strip along the soffit.
Ridge ventilation

Cross-ventilation requires an exit as well as an entry. A ridge vent tile or a continuous ridge ventilator at the apex provides the exit. Check whether the existing ridge is fully bedded or has a vent tile: a fully bedded ridge with no exit means the ventilation path ends at a dead end and air will not circulate. Adding ridge ventilation is a roofing job; it typically requires removing a section of ridge tiles, bedding in a ventilated ridge unit, and re-pointing.
Warm roof option

The alternative to a cold roof is a warm roof: insulating at rafter level rather than at ceiling level. The roof space is removed and the insulation sits close to the roof deck. No cross-ventilation is needed because the insulation keeps the deck near room temperature and stops condensation. A warm roof needs a different product (PIR board or high-performance mineral wool batts) and a vapour control layer on the warm side. A separate tutorial covers the warm-roof method for loft conversions.
